one_line_takeaway: Universal, actor-critic-optimized poisoning payloads can reliably cause LLM assistants to write fabricated persistent memories from adversarial external documents (up to 99.8% injection rate on GPT-5.5), and these dormant memories are retrieved and steer attacker-intended behavior in later, unrelated conversations 41-74% of the time end-to-end for goal-adjacent queries, while current prompt-based defenses remain brittle against adaptive variants of the attack.
key_finding: The Actor-Critic attack substantially outperforms the User Review baseline, achieving near-100% IR for GPT-5.4/GPT-5.5 in the tool-based regime on the Behavior subset (99.4% and 99.8% respectively) versus below 5% for User Review. Injection rates were generally lower on Agent Action than Behavior, and Claude Sonnet 4.6 was the most robust model (e.g., only 6.5% IR on Agent Action, tool-based), attributed partly to more frequent explicit refusals rather than silent ignoring. Post-injection, retrieval and usage were strongly proximity-dependent: goal-adjacent Retrieval Rate ranged 90-95% (Behavior) and 94-98% (Agent Action) versus goal-distant RR of only 3-8% and 13-18% respectively. Adversarial Usage Rate (conditional on retrieval) for goal-adjacent queries ranged 42-85% on Behavior and 60-89% on Agent Action (Claude lowest at 60%, Gemini highest at 89%), while goal-distant AUR dropped to 0-6% (Behavior) and 6-17% (Agent Action). Fully coupled end-to-end success (single-attack, external-manager retrieval) for goal-adjacent queries ranged 41.0-73.9% on Behavior and 3.0-66.0% on Agent Action, versus 0-1% and 0-5% for goal-distant. Manual validation on production interfaces: 24/25 successes for ChatGPT-5.4 and Claude-Sonnet-4.6, 22/25 (88.0%) for Kimi-K2.6. Defenses: GEPA Prompt Hardening and Hardening+Spotlighting drove IR near zero for Claude-Sonnet-4.6 and Gemini-3.1 but remained brittle elsewhere -- under GEPA hardening, the adaptive AC+ attack raised IR from 6.2% to 64.6% on Kimi-K2.6 and from 0.4% to 21.8% on DeepSeek-v4. Mechanistically, activation probes distinguished injected from benign documents with AUROC 0.93-0.99 (single-layer) and often >0.95 (fused multi-layer); cross-model transfer via shared PCA was near-random (0.48-0.54 AUROC) but orthogonal Procrustes alignment recovered 0.74-0.85 AUROC, approaching native-probe performance of 0.77-0.88. Successful injections consistently received higher attention mass on the payload than failed ones (e.g., Gemma-4-26B: 0.8 vs 0.5 x10^-3; Qwen-3.6-35B: 1.4 vs 0.8 x10^-3), though attention alone was not sufficient (GPT-OSS-20B attended to payloads but had zero successful injections on its subset).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'sleeper memory poisoning': a delayed attack against memory-augmented LLM assistants in which an attacker manipulates external context (a document, webpage, email, repo, etc.) so that the assistant writes a fabricated memory about the user, which then lies dormant and later surfaces in an unrelated future conversation to steer the assistant's behavior. The authors formalize a three-stage attacker objective (Injection, Retrieval, Adversarial usage), build a two-stage universal attack: (1) an LLM-driven actor-critic search (adapted from Shi et al. 2025a) that optimizes a reusable payload template Padv to maximize memory-write injection, and (2) retrieval-aware memory-goal rewriting that paraphrases the adversarial memory to maximize embedding similarity to plausible future queries, increasing the odds a semantic-retrieval memory manager surfaces it later. They evaluate the full pipeline end-to-end (injection, retrieval, usage) across six proprietary/open-weight LLMs (GPT-5.4, GPT-5.5, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Kimi-K2.6, DeepSeek-V4-Pro), two memory-management regimes (tool-based/model-managed vs. external-manager e.g. Mem0-style), and two curated datasets (a 700-sample injection dataset with Behavior and Agent Action subsets, and a 400-goal post-injection dataset). They also test four prompt-based defenses plus a document scanner, and run a mechanistic analysis (activation probing and attention analysis) of what internally distinguishes successful injections.
threat_model: Black-box, external-content adversary with no access to model weights, system prompt, the memory-writing/retrieval mechanism, or the contents of the user's memory bank M, and no presence in any future session where memory might be retrieved. The attacker can only influence the assistant through user-provided context d (e.g., a document, webpage, email, code repository, transcript) that becomes part of a user input U = (q, d_adv), where d_adv = d ⊕ P_adv(m_adv): an adversarial payload P_adv designed to induce the memory-writer W to store the attacker's target memory m_adv as if it were a legitimate user memory. Attack success requires three conjunctive conditions across separate sessions: (1) Injection — m_adv is written to memory M; (2) Retrieval — m_adv is retrieved as context for a later, unrelated user input U'; (3) Adversarial usage — the generation model conditions on the retrieved memory and produces attacker-aligned behavior instead of reflecting true user intent. The paper explicitly frames this as universal (a single reusable payload template combinable with many adversarial goals across many unseen documents), distinguishing it from prior work assuming direct corpus access, multi-turn interaction, or white-box optimization.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) injection and post-injection stages are evaluated in decoupled fashion in the main text (to avoid confounding low-IR models with high-variance retrieval/usage estimates), with fully coupled end-to-end rates relegated to an appendix, so headline per-stage numbers should not be read as standalone deployment risk without the coupled figures. (2) Attack generation targets injection and retrieval explicitly but does not directly optimize for downstream adversarial usage -- usage is only measured, not optimized against. (3) System prompts used were 'tailored to the model' rather than uniformly the exact deployed prompts in all cases (noted in the truncated abstract fragment as relying in some settings on leaked, reconstructed, or empirically inferred prompts rather than exact deployed ones), which could bias comparisons across models/regimes. (4) Prompt-based defenses were shown to be inconsistent across models and vulnerable to adaptive attacks (AC+), i.e., no defense evaluated provides model-agnostic, adaptation-robust protection. (5) Mechanistic activation-probing detection requires white-box access to model internals, which is not available for many production/proprietary deployments. (6) Manual production-interface validation was limited to a small sample (25 cases per model on ChatGPT-5.4/Claude-Sonnet-4.6, 25 on Kimi-K2.6).

datasets_used: Custom Memory Injection Dataset: 700 (document, goal) pairs from 15 document sources (news articles, legal filings, code, scientific articles, patents, financial transcripts, and other realistic external contexts), split into Behavior (500 samples) and Agent Action (200 samples) subsets

Custom post-injection evaluation dataset: 400 goals sampled from the injection dataset (200 Behavior, 200 Agent Action), split evenly into goal-adjacent and goal-distant future-session queries, run in new conversation sessions with the original adversarial document removed
Simulated agentic environment for Agent Action future-session tasks, built with OpenClaw
quantitative_results: Injection Rate (Actor-Critic, tool-based, LLM Behavior): 99.4% GPT-5.4, 99.8% GPT-5.5, 64.2% Sonnet-4.6, 88.6% Gemini-3.1, 95.0% Kimi-K2.6, 96.2% DeepSeek-v4
Injection Rate (User Review baseline, tool-based, LLM Behavior): 3.0% GPT-5.4, 4.2% GPT-5.5, 0.0% Sonnet-4.6, 62.4% Gemini-3.1, 62.8% Kimi-K2.6, 79.0% DeepSeek-v4
Injection Rate (Actor-Critic, external-manager, LLM Behavior): 80.4% GPT-5.4, 75.0% GPT-5.5, 13.6% Sonnet-4.6, 54.2% Gemini-3.1, 78.8% Kimi-K2.6, 86.4% DeepSeek-v4
Injection Rate (Actor-Critic, tool-based, Agent Action): 97.0% GPT-5.4, 91.5% GPT-5.5, 6.5% Sonnet-4.6, 67.0% Gemini-3.1, 81.0% Kimi-K2.6, 88.5% DeepSeek-v4
Retrieval Rate goal-adjacent vs goal-distant (LLM Behavior, external manager): 90-95% vs 3-8% across models
Retrieval Rate goal-adjacent vs goal-distant (Agent Action, external manager): 94-98% vs 13-18% across models
Adversarial Usage Rate goal-adjacent (LLM Behavior): 42.0% GPT-5.4 to 85.0% DeepSeek-v4; goal-distant: 0.0-6.0%
Adversarial Usage Rate goal-adjacent (Agent Action): 60.0% Sonnet-4.6 to 89.0% Gemini-3.1; goal-distant: 6.0-17.0%
End-to-end (single-attack, external-manager retrieval, goal-adjacent): 41.0-73.9% on LLM Behavior, 3.0-66.0% on Agent Action; goal-distant: 0.0-1.0% and 0.0-5.0% respectively
Production web-interface validation: 24/25 successes for ChatGPT-5.4 (extended thinking) and Claude-Sonnet-4.6; 22/25 (88.0%) for Kimi-K2.6 (12/13 = 92.3% Behavior, 10/12 = 83.3% Agent Action)
Defense (GEPA Prompt Hardening) IR under adaptive attack AC+ vs base AC (LLM Behavior): Kimi-K2.6 rises from 6.2% to 64.6%; DeepSeek-v4 rises from 0.4% to 21.8%
Activation probe detection: single-layer PCA-reduced QDA probes achieve AUROC 0.93-0.99 across six models; fused multi-layer probes often exceed 0.95 AUROC
Best LLM Document Scanner (Gemma-4-26B) achieves localization score above 0.96
Attention mass gap (success vs failure), scaled by 10^3: Gemma-4-26B 0.8 vs 0.5; Qwen-3.6-35B 1.4 vs 0.8

Cross-model activation-signature transfer: shared PCA alignment near-random (0.48-0.54 AUROC); orthogonal Procrustes alignment recovers 0.74-0.85 AUROC, approaching target-native probe performance of 0.77-0.88 AUROC
baselines_compared: User Review attack (adapted from Raghav and Choong 2026): HTML injection placing the adversarial goal inside a fabricated user review appended to the document
No-defense baseline (undefended models, Table 1) used to measure the effect of Naive Prompt Hardening, GEPA Prompt Hardening, Extreme Spotlighting, and Hardening+Spotlighting defenses
Adaptive attack variant AC+ (actor-critic regenerated with access to the defended prompt format) compared against base AC attack under each defense
